ppt.title: Life Processes - Session 1
ppt.presentationTitle: Understanding Life Processes: Nutrition and Respiration
ppt.presentationGoal: To enable teachers to deliver a complete, engaging lesson on the fundamental life processes of nutrition and respiration without external references.

licensingNotes: Use Wikimedia Commons images by default and use in-app SVG/native slide text only as a fallback.
assetSearchPlan.fallbackStrategy: Use SVG diagrams with editable labels if specific Wikimedia images are unavailable.

licenseChecklist: Verify all image sources allow commercial/educational reuse.
Ensure attribution text is accurate and complete.
Confirm no copyrighted characters or logos appear in visuals.
presentationWarnings: Do not introduce concepts from future sessions (e.g., detailed cellular respiration chemistry).
Maintain focus on macroscopic processes for this session only.
Ensure all diagrams are clearly labeled to avoid confusion with microscopic structures.

learningOutcomesCovered: Define life processes and their necessity for survival.
Explain the autotrophic mode of nutrition in plants.
Describe the process of photosynthesis including inputs and outputs.
Understand basic respiration as energy release.
taughtConceptsCovered: Sunlight, CO2, and water as inputs for photosynthesis

slides.teachingBeat: Set the stage for the lesson and establish relevance.
mustTeach: How do organisms survive without food?
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Life Processes: Nutrition & Respiration
> Life Processes: Nutrition and Respiration
> What makes something 'alive'?
- How do organisms survive without food?

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: title_identity -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Set the stage for the lesson and establish relevance.
Purpose: Introduce the session topic.
Explain: Start by asking students what they think keeps them alive. Discuss that even when we sleep, our bodies are working hard to keep us going.
Ask: 'If you didn't eat for a week, would you still be alive? Why or why not?'
Listen for: Answers about energy stores, water needs, and breathing.
Transition: Today we will explore the specific processes that allow all living things to survive.
Explain: How do organisms survive without food?
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Set the stage for the lesson and establish relevance.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students will know today's focus is on how living things get energy.
bulletPoints: How do organisms survive without food?
speakerNotes: Purpose: Set the stage for the lesson and establish relevance.
Purpose: Introduce the session topic.
Explain: Start by asking students what they think keeps them alive. Discuss that even when we sleep, our bodies are working hard to keep us going.
Ask: 'If you didn't eat for a week, would you still be alive? Why or why not?'
Listen for: Answers about energy stores, water needs, and breathing.
Transition: Today we will explore the specific processes that allow all living things to survive.
Explain: How do organisms survive without food?

slides.teachingBeat: Connect prior knowledge of eating food to the biological concept of nutrition.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Why Do We Need Food?
> Food = Energy + Raw Materials
> Energy for movement and thinking
- Building blocks for growth
- Repairing damaged tissues

<!-- layout: visual-first -->
<!-- purpose: lesson_hook -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Connect prior knowledge of eating food to the biological concept of nutrition.
Purpose: Hook students with the fundamental question of why we eat.
Explain: Clarify that 'nutrition' isn't just about eating; it's a biological process. We need energy to run, think, and grow.
Ask: 'What happens if you don't get enough food? How does your body feel?'
Listen for: Fatigue, weakness, inability to concentrate.
Correct: Ensure students understand that even plants need a form of nutrition (photosynthesis) just like animals do.
Board work: Write 'Energy' and 'Raw Materials' on the board as key takeaways.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Connect prior knowledge of eating food to the biological concept of nutrition.
slides.studentTakeaway: Food is not just for taste; it provides energy and raw materials.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Connect prior knowledge of eating food to the biological concept of nutrition.
Purpose: Hook students with the fundamental question of why we eat.
Explain: Clarify that 'nutrition' isn't just about eating; it's a biological process. We need energy to run, think, and grow.
Ask: 'What happens if you don't get enough food? How does your body feel?'
Listen for: Fatigue, weakness, inability to concentrate.
Correct: Ensure students understand that even plants need a form of nutrition (photosynthesis) just like animals do.
Board work: Write 'Energy' and 'Raw Materials' on the board as key takeaways.
slides.visualPlan: Simple icon-based graphic showing a plate of food transforming into energy sparks (lightning bolt) and building blocks (bricks).

assets.purpose: Illustrate the dual purpose of nutrition
assets.altText: Students are conducting a hands-on science experiment in a classroom, using lemons and oranges to explore concepts in a practical way. The atmosphere is lively and engaging.
assets.placementHint: Right side of the slide, next to bullet points.

animationHints: Animate icons appearing one by one as each point is discussed

slides.teachingBeat: Introduce the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
mustTeach: Autotrophs: Make their own food (e.g., Plants)
Heterotrophs: Depend on others for food (e.g., Animals)
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Two Ways to Get Food
> Self-Makers vs. Dependent Eaters
> Autotrophs: Make their own food (e.g., Plants)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: core_concept -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Introduce the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
Purpose: Define the two main categories of nutrition.
Explain: Use a clear distinction. Autotrophs are 'self-feeders' (auto = self, trophos = feeder). Heterotrophs rely on others.
Ask: 'Can you name an animal that makes its own food? What about a plant?'
Listen for: Confusion or correct answers. Clarify that no animals make their own food from sunlight directly (unlike plants).
Transition: Let's look closer at how the self-makers do it.
Explain: Student takeaway: Plants make their own food; animals must eat others.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Introduce the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
slides.studentTakeaway: Plants make their own food; animals must eat others.
onSlideText: Autotrophs: Make their own food (e.g., Plants)
Heterotrophs: Depend on others for food (e.g., Animals)
speakerNotes: Purpose: Introduce the two fundamental modes of nutrition.
Purpose: Define the two main categories of nutrition.
Explain: Use a clear distinction. Autotrophs are 'self-feeders' (auto = self, trophos = feeder). Heterotrophs rely on others.
Ask: 'Can you name an animal that makes its own food? What about a plant?'
Listen for: Confusion or correct answers. Clarify that no animals make their own food from sunlight directly (unlike plants).
Transition: Let's look closer at how the self-makers do it.
Explain: Student takeaway: Plants make their own food; animals must eat others.

slides.teachingBeat: Detail the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
mustTeach: Sunlight + Water + Carbon Dioxide -> Glucose + Oxygen
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# How Plants Make Food: Photosynthesis
> The Photosynthesis Equation (Simplified)
> Sunlight + Water + Carbon Dioxide -> Glucose + Oxygen

<!-- layout: board-example -->
<!-- purpose: worked_example -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Detail the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
Purpose: Explain the core mechanism of photosynthesis without complex chemistry.
Explain: Break down the inputs. Sunlight is captured by chlorophyll (green pigment). Water comes from roots, CO2 from air through stomata.
Ask: 'Where does the oxygen we breathe come from? Is it just a waste product for plants?'
Listen for: Answers about respiration or photosynthesis output. Clarify that while plants use some oxygen, they release excess which is vital for us.
Board work: Draw a simple leaf diagram labeling 'Sunlight', 'Water (roots)', and 'CO2 (leaves)' as inputs.
Explain: Student takeaway: Plants use sunlight, water, and CO2 to make glucose and oxygen.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Detail the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
slides.studentTakeaway: Plants use sunlight, water, and CO2 to make glucose and oxygen.
onSlideText: Sunlight + Water + Carbon Dioxide -> Glucose + Oxygen
speakerNotes: Purpose: Detail the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis.
Purpose: Explain the core mechanism of photosynthesis without complex chemistry.
Explain: Break down the inputs. Sunlight is captured by chlorophyll (green pigment). Water comes from roots, CO2 from air through stomata.
Ask: 'Where does the oxygen we breathe come from? Is it just a waste product for plants?'
Listen for: Answers about respiration or photosynthesis output. Clarify that while plants use some oxygen, they release excess which is vital for us.
Board work: Draw a simple leaf diagram labeling 'Sunlight', 'Water (roots)', and 'CO2 (leaves)' as inputs.
Explain: Student takeaway: Plants use sunlight, water, and CO2 to make glucose and oxygen.
slides.visualPlan: Clear flowchart showing a plant with arrows pointing in from Sun, Water, Air, and an arrow out to Glucose (food) and Oxygen.

assets.purpose: Illustrate the inputs and outputs of photosynthesis
assets.placementHint: Center of slide, large enough to be read by all students.

animationHints: Animate arrows appearing sequentially: Sun -> Water/CO2 -> Plant -> Glucose/Oxygen

slides.teachingBeat: Assess immediate understanding of key concepts.
mustTeach: 2. Name one input needed for photosynthesis.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Quick Check: Life Processes
> Can you answer these?
> 1. What is an autotroph?
- 2. Name one input needed for photosynthesis.

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: quick_assessment -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Assess immediate understanding of key concepts.
Purpose: Quick formative assessment.
Ask: 'Who can tell me what an autotroph is?'
Listen for: Definition (makes own food). Follow up with the second question about photosynthesis inputs.
Correct: If a student says 'soil' as a primary input, clarify that water and CO2 are the key chemical inputs, though soil provides nutrients.
Transition: Great job! Now let's briefly touch on how we get energy from food.
Explain: 2. Name one input needed for photosynthesis.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Assess immediate understanding of key concepts.
slides.studentTakeaway: Students can identify the difference between autotrophs and heterotrophs.
bulletPoints: 2. Name one input needed for photosynthesis.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Assess immediate understanding of key concepts.
Purpose: Quick formative assessment.
Ask: 'Who can tell me what an autotroph is?'
Listen for: Definition (makes own food). Follow up with the second question about photosynthesis inputs.
Correct: If a student says 'soil' as a primary input, clarify that water and CO2 are the key chemical inputs, though soil provides nutrients.
Transition: Great job! Now let's briefly touch on how we get energy from food.
Explain: 2. Name one input needed for photosynthesis.

slides.teachingBeat: Summarize key points and bridge to the next session.
mustTeach: Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat it.
slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Summary: Life Processes Continue...
> Key Takeaways
> Life processes keep us alive.
- Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat it.
- Photosynthesis uses sunlight, water, CO2.

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: summary -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Summarize key points and bridge to the next session.
Purpose: Consolidate learning and set up the next session.
Explain: Briefly recap the three main points. Emphasize that respiration (getting energy from food) is the next step we will explore in detail.
Ask: 'What do you think happens to the glucose plants make? How does it become energy for us?'
Listen for: Ideas about digestion or burning fuel.
Transition: In our next session, we'll dive into how this food is actually used by cells. Don't forget your homework!
Explain: Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat it.
-->
slides.teacherIntent: Summarize key points and bridge to the next session.
slides.studentTakeaway: Recap of nutrition types and a teaser for respiration details.
bulletPoints: Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat it.
speakerNotes: Purpose: Summarize key points and bridge to the next session.
Purpose: Consolidate learning and set up the next session.
Explain: Briefly recap the three main points. Emphasize that respiration (getting energy from food) is the next step we will explore in detail.
Ask: 'What do you think happens to the glucose plants make? How does it become energy for us?'
Listen for: Ideas about digestion or burning fuel.
Transition: In our next session, we'll dive into how this food is actually used by cells. Don't forget your homework!
Explain: Autotrophs make food; Heterotrophs eat it.

assets.attributionText: Andrea Miranda Paez, Kayleigh Chalkowski, Sarah Zohdy & Janna R. Willoughby.
assets.altText: The avian malaria life cycle starts when (a) a feeding mosquito infects an avian host with Plasmodium sporozoites; (b) sporozoites then develop into exo-erythrocytic meronts (i.e. cryptozoites) in reticuloendothelial cells (e.g. spleen, liver, bone) throughout the body (c) followed by the development of merozoites into the second pre-erythrocytic exo-erythrytic stage and producing metacryptozoites. d Following development into metacryptozoites, further generations of this stage can be formed from previous generations. e Alternatively, merozoites from metacrytpzoites can enter the bloodstream, infect erythrocytes and (f) become meronts to continue into the erythritic cycle. g Merozoites can also develop into the next exo-erythrocytic form, the post-erythritic phanerozoites, which can (h) also develop further generations of phanerozoites or (i) develop into merozoites. Merozoites can be formed from either phanerozoites or erythrocytic meronts. From (j) merozoites, the erythrocytic cycle continues with the development of (k) male and female micro/macrogametocytes. These gametocytes are then (l) capable of infecting another mosquito to begin the process of sporogony in this next host. Once inside the mosquito, the gametocytes develop into (m) gametes in the midgut. These gametes come together to form (n) a zygote, which then develops into ookinetes that travel from the midgut to the epithelium, (o) followed by development into oocysts. Within the oocysts, (p) infective haploid sporozoites form and burst through the oocyst wall once reaching maturity. These haploid sporozoites then invade mosquito salivary glands, where they can be transferred to another bird host when the mosquito feeds.

mustTeach: Homework: List 3 autotrophs and 3 heterotrophs you see at home/school.

slides.presentonMarkdown:
# Your Task: Observe Life Processes
> Ready for the next step?
> Homework: List 3 autotrophs and 3 heterotrophs you see at home/school.
- Next Session: How does food become energy? (Respiration)

<!-- layout: concept-split -->
<!-- purpose: homework_next_session -->

<!-- speaker-notes
Purpose: Assign homework and preview the next topic.
Purpose: Clear assignment and forward-looking statement.
Explain: Ensure students understand they need to observe their environment. Remind them that respiration is about energy release, which we will cover in depth later.
Ask: 'Who can guess what happens if a plant doesn't get sunlight for photosynthesis?'
Listen for: Answers about lack of food or growth issues.
Transition: Great thinking! See you next time to unlock the mystery of energy.
Explain: Next Session: How does food become energy? (Respiration)
-->
